Name: spectral_geometry_mixed_precision
Title: Spectral Geometry for Training-Free Mixed Precision Quantization
Short Hypothesis: Leveraging the spectral properties of model representations can improve the efficiency and accuracy of training-free mixed precision quantization methods, providing a more deterministic approach compared to existing heuristic methods.
Related Work: While several recent works focus on training-free mixed precision quantization (e.g., EMQ, InfoQ), they primarily rely on heuristic-based proxies or evolutionary algorithms. This proposal distinguishes itself by directly utilizing the spectral properties of the model's parameter space, which has been underexplored in existing literature, to create a more principled and theoretically grounded approach to mixed precision quantization.
Abstract: This research proposes a novel training-free mixed precision quantization framework that leverages the geometric spectral properties of deep learning model representations. By analyzing the curvature of the loss landscape and the eigenvalues of the Hessian matrix, we can derive quantization strategies that preserve model accuracy while minimizing computational overhead. This framework will utilize deterministic spectral estimators to identify critical network layers and assign optimal bit-widths based on their geometric characteristics, enhancing the correlation between quantization and performance metrics. We will evaluate our approach against state-of-the-art mixed precision methods across various architectures and datasets, demonstrating superior efficiency and accuracy without the need for extensive retraining or heuristic tuning.
Experiments: 1. Implement a baseline mixed precision quantization method for comparison.
2. Develop and apply the spectral geometry-based quantization framework to various well-known architectures (e.g., ResNet, ViT).
3. Evaluate performance using benchmarks like ImageNet and COCO, measuring accuracy, computational efficiency, and memory usage.
4. Assess the correlation between the proposed spectral metrics and quantization error to validate the effectiveness of the geometric approach.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Potential overfitting to specific architectures or datasets.
The complexity of accurately estimating spectral properties in high-dimensional spaces could introduce noise.
Limited generalizability if the geometric properties do not hold across different model types.
